claims.flip_evidence_text: except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate).
claims.notes: Clause (c) (changes in industry conditions) falls within the scope of the disproportionate impact carve-back because it is among clauses (a)–(e).

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any effect, event, development or change that, individually or in the aggregate with all other effects, events, developments or changes, is, or would reasonably be expected to be, materially adverse to (i) the business, results of operations or financial condition, assets of the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole or (ii) the ability of the Company or Hospitality to consummate the Mergers before the End Date; provided, however, that in the case of clause (i), no effect, event, development or change resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or relating to any of the following shall be deemed to be or constitute a “Company Material Adverse Effect,” and no effect, event, development or change (by itself or when aggregated or taken together with any and all other such effects, events, developments or changes) to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to, or related to any of the following shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur: (a) general economic conditions (or changes in such conditions) in the United States or any region thereof or any other country or region in the world, or conditions in the global economy generally; (b) conditions (or changes in such conditions) in the securities markets, credit markets, currency markets or other financial markets in the United States or any region thereof or any other country or region in the world, including (i) changes in interest rates in the United States or any other country or region in the world and changes in exchange rates for the currencies of any countries and (ii) any suspension of trading in securities (whether equity, debt, derivative or hybrid securities) generally on any securities exchange or over-the-counter market operating in the United States or any region thereof or any other country or region in the world; (c) conditions (or changes in such conditions) in any of the industries in which the Company and or its Subsidiaries conduct business; (d) political conditions (or changes in such conditions) in the United States or any region thereof or any other country or region in the world or acts of war, sabotage or terrorism (including any escalation or general worsening of any such acts of war, sabotage or terrorism) in the United States or any region thereof or any other country or region in the world; (e) any Force Majeure event; (f) the announcement of this Agreement or the pendency or consummation of the transactions contemplated hereby, including (i) the identity of Parent and its affiliates and (ii) the impact thereof on relationships, contractual or otherwise, with customers, suppliers, licensors, distributors, partners or employees (provided that this clause (f) shall not apply to any representation or warranty in Section 5.5 and, solely to the extent related thereto, the condition set forth in Section 8.2(a)); (g) the taking of any action expressly required by this Agreement or with the consent of Parent in writing ; (h) changes in law or other legal or regulatory conditions (or the interpretation thereof), or changes in GAAP or other accounting standards (or the interpretation thereof), in each case, after the date hereof; (i) changes in the stock price of the Paired Common Shares or the trading volume of the Paired Common Shares, or any failure by the Company or Hospitality to meet any public estimates or expectations of the revenue, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations of the Company or Hospitality for any period, or any failure by any Paired Entity or Paired Entities Subsidiary to meet any internal budgets, plans or forecasts of its revenues, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations (but not, in each case, the underlying cause of such changes or failures, unless such changes or failures would otherwise be excepted from this definition); or (j) any litigation by the stockholders of the Paired Entities related to this Agreement, the Mergers or other transactions contemplated hereby; except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate). (Page 86)

Question:
Change in Target's industry: subject to "disproportionate impact" modifier

Llama answer:
A change in Target's industry is subject to the "disproportionate impact" modifier, meaning that it will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it disproportionately adversely affects the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business.

Canonical claims:
- claim-56a421c808db: A change in Target's industry is subject to the "disproportionate impact" modifier, meaning that it will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it disproportionately adversely affects the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

 Contract excerpt defines "Company Material Adverse Effect" (CMAE). It lists exceptions (a)-(j) that are not deemed CMAE and not taken into account when determining CMAE. Then there's an exception to the exceptions: "except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company..."

 Let's map the clauses:
 - (a) general economic conditions
 - (b) securities/financial markets
 - (c) conditions in any of the industries in which the Company and/or its Subsidiaries conduct business
 - (d) political conditions, war, terrorism
 - (e) Force Majeure
 - (f) announcement of agreement
 - (g) action required by agreement or with Parent consent
 - (h) changes in law/GAAP
 - (i) stock price/trading volume, failure to meet estimates
 - (j) litigation by stockholders

 The disproportionate impact modifier applies to clauses (a) through (e) and (h). It does NOT apply to (f), (g), (i), or (j).

 The claim says: "A change in Target's industry is subject to the 'disproportionate impact' modifier, meaning that it will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it disproportionately adversely affects the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business."

 Target's industry change corresponds to clause (c). Clause (c) is within the set (a)-(e) and (h) to which the disproportionate impact modifier applies. So yes, a change in the industry is subject to the disproportionate impact modifier.

 Wait, let me read carefully. The claim says "A change in Target's industry is subject to the 'disproportionate impact' modifier". Clause (c) is "conditions (or changes in such conditions) in any of the industries in which the Company and or its Subsidiaries conduct business". This is indeed covered by the disproportionate impact carve-back: "except to the extent such effects... to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect...".

 So the claim is true.

 Now, is it load-bearing? The claim is essentially a direct paraphrase of the contract language regarding clause (c) and the disproportionate impact modifier. The modifier is the key exception that brings back into consideration effects that would otherwise be excluded. The claim captures this. The "flip_evidence_text" would be the part of the contract that shows the disproportionate impact language applies to (a)-(e) and (h), which includes (c). If the claim were false, the flip evidence would be the text showing it applies only to (a)-(e) and (h), implying (c) is included. Wait, the question asks for flip evidence if load-bearing=yes. Flip evidence is the text that would refute the claim if the claim were false. Actually, looking at the instructions from previous similar tasks: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". The flip evidence is the specific text in the contract that determines the truth value of the claim. It's the evidence that if flipped (i.e., interpreted differently or if it said something else) would change the label.

 Actually, in the context of these annotation tasks, "load-bearing" usually means the claim is substantive and the flip evidence is the specific contractual text that supports or refutes it. Let me think about what "flip_evidence_text" specifically means here. In some annotation schemes, it's the minimal span of text that, if its meaning were flipped (or if it were absent), would change the correctness of the claim. It should be the exact text from the contract excerpt.

 The claim is about the disproportionate impact modifier applying to industry changes. The relevant text is: "except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate)."

 And clause (c) is: "(c) conditions (or changes in such conditions) in any of the industries in which the Company and or its Subsidiaries conduct business;"

 So the flip evidence should probably be the exception-to-the-exceptions clause, specifically mentioning (a) through (e) and (h), since that includes (c).

 Is the claim load-bearing? Yes, it's a substantive claim about the legal effect of the contract.

 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: The text showing the disproportionate impact applies to (a)-(e) and (h). Since (c) is industry conditions, it's included. The exact text: "except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate)."
 notes: empty or brief.

 Wait, I need to be careful. The claim says "meaning that it will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it disproportionately adversely affects...". Is that fully accurate? The modifier says that if it disproportionately adversely affects, then such adverse effects shall be taken into account. It doesn't explicitly say it "will only be considered a CMAE if...". Rather, the default is that industry changes (clause c) are NOT taken into account. The exception is that if disproportionate, they ARE taken into account. So the claim is essentially saying: industry changes are excluded unless disproportionate. That is true.

 But is the phrasing "will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if" slightly off? The definition says such effects are not deemed CMAE and not taken into account, EXCEPT to the extent they disproportionately adversely affect... So yes, they are only considered (taken into account) if disproportionate. The claim is true.

 Let me double-check if there's any subtlety. The claim says "A change in Target's industry is subject to the 'disproportionate impact' modifier". Clause (c) is indeed subject to it because (c) is in (a)-(e). True.

 What about the phrase "as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business"? That matches the text.

 So:
 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Company Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be likely to occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate)."
 notes: Clause (c) (industry conditions) is within the scope of the disproportionate impact carve-back for clauses (a)-(e) and (h).

 Actually, the flip evidence should probably be concise. I'll provide the key sentence.

 Let me reconsider "load_bearing". In some contexts, a claim is load-bearing if it's a central point that the answer hinges on, not just a trivial restatement. This claim is the core of the answer. So yes.

 One more check: The question is "Change in Target's industry: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier". The Llama answer is the claim text. The canonical claim is identical to the Llama answer. So I'm evaluating whether this claim is correct based on the contract.

 Yes, it's correct.

 Final output format should be something like:
 claim-56a421c808db:
   correctness_label: true
   load_bearing_label: yes
   flip_evidence_text: "except to the extent such effects, events, developments or changes to the extent resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (a) through (e) and (h) disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies that conduct business in the United States and in the industries in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, such adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account ... solely to the extent they are disproportionate)."
   notes: ""

 I should make sure to include the claim ID.

 The user said "For each claim return: ...". So I'll format it clearly.

 Wait, is there any chance the claim is partially true because the modifier applies to (a)-(e) and (h), but the claim doesn't mention that it also applies to (h)? No, the claim is specifically about industry changes (which is (c)), and says it is subject to the modifier. That is true. It doesn't need to mention (h).

 Is it possible that the claim is false because the modifier applies to the extent resulting from (a)-(e) and (h), but the claim says "meaning that it will only be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if..."? The text says "shall be taken into account". Being taken into account is necessary for being considered a CMAE, but not sufficient (it still has to be materially adverse, etc.). However, in the context of the carve-outs, if it's excluded from being taken into account, it cannot be a CMAE. So if it is taken into account, it can be considered. The "only if" phrasing is acceptable in this context.

 I'll go with true.
